---
title: "Statistics by Intuition"
subtitle: "A field notebook for scientists — the intuition behind the statistics and machine learning you'll be asked about, and the practice to make it stick."
toc: false
---

::: {.field-notes-band}
<div class="fn-eyebrow">Field notebook · statistics & ML for scientists</div>
<div class="fn-title">Understand it first. Compute it second. Defend it in the interview.</div>
<div class="fn-meta">
  <span class="fn-tag">Intuition-first</span>
  <span class="fn-tag">Interview-ready</span>
  <span class="fn-tag">Interactive figures</span>
  <span class="fn-tag">Primary literature</span>
</div>
:::

Most statistics education hands you a formula and hopes the meaning arrives
later. It usually doesn't. This site inverts the order: every topic starts
with a problem a scientist actually has — a suspicious test result, a batch
effect, a model that looks great on training data and collapses in the wild —
and lets the mathematics emerge from the problem rather than the other way
around.

It is built for two people who happen to be the same person on different days:

- **The learner**, building durable intuition for the concepts that quietly
  underpin all of quantitative biology and machine learning.
- **The candidate**, three days from a technical interview, who needs to
  explain what a *p*-value actually is, choose the right normalization, read a
  residual plot, or reason out loud about why a model is overfitting — and
  wants to have *seen the question before*.

Throughout, one lens keeps the material honest: **what would a technical
interviewer — say, on a research science team building frontier models — press
you on?** Not trivia. The reasoning: *why this method, what does it assume,
how would you know if it broke, and what would you plot to find out?*

## How to use this

Each lesson is a small, self-contained unit with the same four parts:

::: {.specimen}
**The anatomy of a lesson**

1. **Lesson plan** — objectives, modules, and the interview questions the
   lesson unlocks.
2. **Background primer** — exactly the prerequisites you need, no more.
3. **In action** — worked examples with numbers you can check by hand, and
   interactive figures where moving a slider *is* the lesson.
4. **Interview problems** — Rosalind-style exercises and the conceptual
   questions an interviewer asks, each with a collapsible model answer.

Read the plan, detour into the primer if anything feels shaky, work the
examples with a pencil, then close the loop on the interview page. The
exercises are where the learning actually happens.
:::

## The curriculum

Five tracks that build on each other, ending where scientific work is heading:
using language models to find patterns in data. See the
[full roadmap](roadmap.qmd) for every planned lesson and how they connect.

::: {.track-grid}
<a class="track-card track-a" href="roadmap.qmd#track-a">
  <div class="tc-eyebrow">Track A</div>
  <div class="tc-title">Inference foundations</div>
  <p class="tc-desc">Bayes, <em>p</em>-values, <em>t</em>-tests, confidence
  intervals, multiple testing, power. The classic interview gauntlet.</p>
  <span class="tc-status">Lesson 1 live</span>
</a>
<a class="track-card track-b" href="roadmap.qmd#track-b">
  <div class="tc-eyebrow">Track B</div>
  <div class="tc-title">Data transformation</div>
  <p class="tc-desc">Z-scores, log/rlog/VST, quantile, min–max. When each is
  right, and what each quietly distorts.</p>
  <span class="tc-status planned">Outlined</span>
</a>
<a class="track-card track-c" href="roadmap.qmd#track-c">
  <div class="tc-eyebrow">Track C</div>
  <div class="tc-title">Regression &amp; models</div>
  <p class="tc-desc">Linear and logistic regression, GLMs, regularization,
  and the bias–variance tradeoff.</p>
  <span class="tc-status planned">Outlined</span>
</a>
<a class="track-card track-d" href="roadmap.qmd#track-d">
  <div class="tc-eyebrow">Track D</div>
  <div class="tc-title">ML fundamentals</div>
  <p class="tc-desc">The ML workflow as decision flowcharts: framing, model
  choice, evaluation, metrics, and diagnostic plots.</p>
  <span class="tc-status planned">Outlined</span>
</a>
<a class="track-card track-e" href="roadmap.qmd#track-e">
  <div class="tc-eyebrow">Track E</div>
  <div class="tc-title">Toward LLMs in science</div>
  <p class="tc-desc">Embeddings, what a language model learns, and using LLMs
  to find patterns in scientific data — the destination.</p>
  <span class="tc-status planned">Outlined</span>
</a>
:::

## Start here

If you're new, begin with **[Lesson 1 — Bayesian Statistics](lessons/01-bayesian-statistics/index.qmd)**.
It teaches the single most useful habit in all of applied statistics — *never
evaluate evidence without asking how common the thing is* — and everything in
the inference track builds on it.

::: {.lens}
Bayesian reasoning is not a niche topic you can skip. Base rates govern why a
"significant" result can still probably be false [@ioannidis2005], why a
sensitive diagnostic can mislead, and why a classifier's precision cratered
when you deployed it on rare events. Interviewers return to it constantly
because it exposes whether you *reason* about uncertainty or merely compute
with it.
:::
